summary.intent: Implement the framework-agnostic ActionGroup core component for GitHub issue #217 in fogodev/ars-ui, following a detailed pre-approved plan including TDD, mutation testing, coverage auditing, and a post-implementation spec audit.
summary.outcome: The ActionGroup agnostic core was fully implemented in crates/ars-components/src/utility/action_group/mod.rs with 49+ unit tests, 13 committed snapshots, spec conformance and proptest coverage, 0 missed mutants across 149 mutants, 1284/1284 line coverage, a post-implementation spec audit landing findings in-diff, and a PR opened against main with Closes #217.

repo: The pre-push gate is `cargo xci-fast`, which runs fmt-check, check, clippy, tests, and a snapshot-count row — all must pass before committing.
Mutation testing uses `cargo mutants -p ars-components -f <file>` per module; missed mutants must be triaged in the same PR — real gaps get tests, true equivalents get `.cargo/mutants.toml` skips.
Snapshot tests use `cargo insta`; `INSTA_UPDATE=always` auto-accepts new snapshots, and `--unreferenced=reject` enforces no orphan fixtures.
The project board is updated via `gh project item-edit` with hardcoded project ID `PVT_kwHOAKgEE84BTlkR`; field and option IDs must be resolved from `gh project field-list` before any status change.
ActionGroup follows the `toggle_group::Machine` structural pattern: `State`, `Event`, `Props`, `Context`, `Effect`, `Part`, and `Api<'a>` with `root_attrs`, `item_attrs`, `overflow_trigger_attrs`.
Item registration order is maintained in a `Vec<Key>` (render order), separate from the `BTreeSet<Key>` used for selected items.
Group-level `disabled` must NOT erase roving focus state — only item-level disabled or explicit navigation should shift `tabindex`; the spec explicitly allows focus/navigation on a disabled group.

The spec sync scope for this task extended to adapter specs in `spec/leptos-components/` and `spec/dioxus-components/` — stale debug-warning requirements and API sketches needed updating alongside the core spec.
Selection Mode::Single with the ActionGroup spec requires that re-selecting the already-selected item clears selection (differs from a typical radio pattern).
Post-implementation audit is a three-phase skill: (1) spec/impl drift section-by-section, (2) two-round 'anything else missing' passes until clean, (3) test-type breadth check.

code.finding: Focus no-op guard (repeated FocusItem on already-focused key) and Blur no-op guard (Blur in Idle state) were mutation survivors until explicit state-unchanged assertions were added — mutation testing is the only reliable way to find these guards.
OverflowChanged with the same count is a no-op; this guard also survived mutation until a test that sent the same count twice and asserted no context change was added.

Previous-focus wrap logic had two arithmetic mutant survivors that required a test for FocusPrev from the first item (wrapping to last enabled) AND a test where the final wrap candidate is disabled (requiring scanning backward past it).
New utility modules must be added to the pub mod list here; the file uses explicit pub mod declarations, not a glob.
Proptest invariants for ActionGroup must be run with `-- --ignored` because the test is annotated #[ignore] to avoid slowing the default test suite.

workflow: Mutation testing runs for a ~1300-line Rust module take 75-90 minutes per pass; plan for 3+ passes when doing targeted remediation.
cargo-mutants emits per-mutant output ONLY on misses — long stretches of silence are normal and do not indicate a hang.
Coverage gaps found by llvm-cov often correlate exactly with mutation survivors: if a guard condition has no test that produces a no-op vs state-change branch, both tools will flag it.

The TDD ordering — failing compile (red), minimal impl (green), hardened tests (mutation/coverage) — is enforced by project conventions, not just preference; the post-implementation audit checks test-type breadth as a phase.
Unrelated formatter drift can appear in files touched by `xci-fast` compilation; always check `git diff` after the gate and restore unintended changes before committing.

The Codex review loop (post-PR) requires inline replies, regression tests for each finding, and re-triggering `@codex review` until a thumbs-up — it is a required gate before the issue auto-closes via the PR merge.

friction: Mutation testing required ~5 hours total across three full passes (initial run, remediation rerun, final clean rerun after coverage edits) with hundreds of polling iterations due to no progress output between misses.
cargo package-cache lock contention from other local Cargo processes caused delays at both the test phase and the xci-fast gate.
An unrelated formatter change in crates/ars-dioxus/tests/attr_passthrough.rs appeared after xci-fast and needed manual revert before committing to keep the PR scope clean.
The initial test scaffold had a function name shadowing compile error that required a patch before the focused tests could run.
The visible-count drift repair path (stale overflow count after unregister) was difficult to test directly because the service's mutable context hook was needed to model externally-drifted state.
The lcov coverage output covered the whole crate by default; a separate awk script was needed to isolate ActionGroup module stats.
open_items: Leptos and Dioxus adapter implementations for ActionGroup are explicitly out of scope for this task — the adapter specs were updated but no adapter code was written.
The Codex review loop has not yet completed — PR is open but @codex review has not been posted and no Codex threads have been resolved.
CI has not yet passed and the issue will remain open until the PR merges with green CI and Codex thumbs-up.
The snapshot-reviewed label needs to be added to the PR (13 new .snap fixtures were committed).
The .cargo/mutants.toml was not modified — if any true equivalent mutants appear in future passes they would need documented skips there.
